---
title: "HW3_MasonHolcombe"
format: html
editor: visual
---

## Mason Holcombe

### Chapter 4

#### 3)

a\) Covariance = 2 and does not 0, not independent

b\) Covariance = 0, so X2 and X3 are independent

c\) Cov(x1,x3) = 0, cov(x2, x3) = 0, so (X1,X2) and X2 are independent

d\) 1/2 \* (cov(x1,x3) and cov(x2, x3)). Both of these are 0, so 1/2 \* 0 = 0. D is independent

e\) cov(x2,x2) = 5, cov(-5/2x1, x2) = -5/2 \* cov(x1,x2) = -5/2 \* -2 = 5, and cov(x2, x3) = 0. So the final covariance is 5 + 5 + 0 = 10 and this does not equal 0. So x2 and (e) are not independent.

#### 4)

a\)

```{r}
mu = c(2,-3,1)
sigma = matrix(c(1,1,1,
                 1,3,2,
                 1,2,2), byrow=T, ncol=3)
a = c(3, -2, 1)
```

```{r}
mean = t(a) %*% mu
var = t(a) %*% sigma %*% a
c(mean, var)
```

3x1 - 2x2 + x2 \~ N(13, 9)

#### 19)

a\) \~ $\chi_6^2$ chi2 with 6 df

b\)

-   p-variate normal with mu as mean and 1/20 \* sigma as covariance matrix

-   p-variate normal with 0 as mean and sigma as covariance matrix

c\) wishart distribution with 19 (20-1) df and sigma as the scale matrix

#### 29)

```{r}
air = read.table("T1-5.DAT")
x5 = air["V5"]
x6 = air["V6"]

X = cbind(x5, x6)
n = nrow(X)
```

a\)

```{r}
xbar = colMeans(X)
S = cov(X)
Sinv = solve(S)

result = numeric(n)
for (i in 1:n){
  xi = matrix(as.numeric(X[i, ]),
              ncol=1)
  result[i] = t(xi - xbar) %*% Sinv %*% (xi - xbar)
}
(result)
```

b\)

```{r}
cutoff = qchisq(0.5, df=2)
mean(result <= cutoff)
```

c\)

```{r}
results_sorted = sort(result)
theoretical_chi2 = qchisq((1:n - (1/2)) / n, df=2)

plot(theoretical_chi2, results_sorted)
abline(0,1,col='red')
```

#### 39)

```{r}
data39 = read.table("T4-6.DAT")
indep = data39["V1"]
supp = data39["V2"]
benev = data39["V3"]
conform = data39["V4"]
leader = data39["V5"]

gender = data39["V6"]
socio = data39["V7"]
```

a\)

Independence - NORMAL

```{r}
hist(indep$V1)
qqnorm(indep$V1)
```

Support -- NOT NORMAL

```{r}
hist(supp$V2)
qqnorm(supp$V2)
```

Benevolence -- NORMAL

```{r}
hist(benev$V3)
qqnorm(benev$V3)
```

Conform - NORMAL

```{r}
hist(conform$V4)
qqnorm(conform$V4)
```

Leader -- NOT NORMAL (SKEWED RIGHT)

```{r}
hist(leader$V5)
qqnorm(leader$V5)
```

b\)

```{r}
varnames = c("V1", "V2", "V3", "V4", "V5")
features = data39[ ,varnames]
```

```{r}
library("MVN")

mvn_result = mvn(data = features, mvn_test = "mardia")
(mvn_result$multivariate_normality)

mvn_result2 = mvn(data = features, mvn_test = "hz")
(mvn_result2$multivariate_normality)
```

c\)

```{r}
support_sqrt = sqrt(supp$V2)

hist(support_sqrt)
qqnorm(support_sqrt)
qqline(support_sqrt)
```

```{r}
leader_log = log(leader$V5)

hist(leader_log)
qqnorm(leader_log)
qqline(leader_log)
```

### Chapter 5

#### 1

a\)

```{r}
n = 4
p = 2

X = matrix(c(2,12,
             8,9,
             6,9,
             8,10), byrow=T, nrow=4, ncol=2)
mu = c(7, 11)

xbar = colMeans(X)
Sinv = solve(cov(X))

T2 = n * t(xbar - mu) %*% Sinv %*% (xbar - mu)
(T2[1,1])
```

b\)

$T^2$ is distributed as $\frac{6}{2}F_{2, 2} = 3 F_{2, 2}$ or $\frac{1}{3} T^2 \sim F_{2,2}$

c\)

```{r}
alpha = 0.05

fobs = (1/3) * T2[1,1]
fcrit = qf(0.05, 2, 2, lower.tail=F)

(fobs > fcrit)

```

**Fail** to reject $H_0: \mu = [7,11]$ at $\alpha=0.05$. The data does not have enough evidence to conclude the mean differs from \[7,11\]

#### 2)

```{r}
C = matrix(c(1, -1,
             1, 1), byrow=T, nrow=2, ncol=2)
CX = t(C %*% t(X))
CXmu = C %*% c(7, 11)


CXbar = colMeans(CX)
CXSinv = solve(cov(CX))

CXT2 = n * t(CXbar - CXmu) %*% CXSinv %*% (CXbar - CXmu)
(CXT2[1,1])
```

#### 18)

```{r}
data18 = read.table("T5-2.DAT")
```

a\)

```{r}
mu0 = c(500, 50, 30)
alpha = 0.05
n = 87
p = 3

xbar18 = colMeans(data18)
Sinv18 = solve(cov(data18))

T2_18 = n * t(xbar18 - mu0) %*% Sinv18 %*% (xbar18 - mu0)

fobs = ((n-p)/(p*(n-1))) * T2_18[1,1]
fcrit = qf(0.05, p, n-p, lower.tail=F)

(fobs > fcrit)

```

**Reject** $H_0: \mu = [500, 50, 30]$ at $\alpha=0.05$. There is strong evidence that $\mu$ differs from \[500, 50, 30\]. This shows that the group of n=87 students scored [differently]{.underline} than the group of thousands of college students which averaged \[500, 50, 30\].

b\)

```{r}
S18 = cov(data18)
eig18 = eigen(S18)

lengths = sqrt(eig18$values) * sqrt(((p*(n-1)) / (n*(n-p))) * fcrit)
directions = eig18$vectors

(lengths)
(directions)
```

c\)

```{r}
feature_names = c("V1", "V2", "V3")

for (i in 1:length(feature_names)){
  qqnorm(data18[[feature_names[i]]], main=feature_names[i])
  qqline(data18[[feature_names[i]]])
}
```

```{r}
pairs(data18)
```

V1 and V3 appear to fall along the theoretical quantiles, whereas V2 has extremes on both ends of the distribution. Looking into the pair plots, I notice a positive covariance among many of the variables. V3 and V1 look the most normally distributed with one another.
